title: Guest Workers and Resistance to U.S. Corporate Despotism By Immanuel Ness University of Illinois Press. 2011. 232 pages. $25 paper
abstract: For nearly fifteen years, the United States has been plagued by major debates regarding the reform of immigration policy. These debates have often been vitriolic and do not appear to be waning anytime soon, as both the public and government officials remain polarized about what reform ought to look like. Interestingly, if there is convergence across the partisan aisles of Washington, DC (if not necessarily among ordinary Americans), it has often been with respect to the expansion of the US guest worker programs. At present, the United States offers temporary employment visas for both highly skilled professionals (including nurses, teachers, and IT professionals) and low-wage workers (specifically in agriculture and hospitality). Some of these visa holders, especially professionals, are eligible for long-term permanent residency (i.e., “green cards”) only upon sponsorship by their employers. Most of these visa holders, however, are eligible to work and live in the United States only on a short-term basis and are expected to return to their home countries upon the completion of the terms of their visas. The expansion of guest worker programs, so the logic goes, addresses immigrant rights advocates' demands for more opportunities for people to legally enter the United States to work while it placates supporters of limits to immigration by extending visas to foreigners for only a limited period of time.
